entry.title: How I Connected Claude AI to My SAP ABAP System Using MCP — A Complete Windows Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP developer community has been buzzing about connecting Claude AI directly to live SAP ABAP systems using MCP (Model Context Protocol) — reading code, modifying objects, creating transports, and activating changes, all from a natural language chat interface.

I tried it myself on Windows, and it works. This blog walks you through exactly how to set it up.

What is MCP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI models like Claude connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — instead of just answering questions from training data, Claude can actually call tools that interact with real systems.

For SAP developers, this means Claude can:

  • Read your actual ABAP code directly from your system
  • Write and modify objects
  • Run syntax checks
  • Create transport requests
  • Activate objects

All without you copy-pasting anything.

The Architecture

 
 
Claude Desktop / Claude Code CLI         ↕ MCP Protocol  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │   mcp-abap-adt           │  ← Read-only (13 tools)  │   mcp-abap-abap-adt-api  │  ← Full CRUD (30 tools)  └──────────────────────────┘         ↕ ADT REST API    Your SAP ABAP System

Both MCP servers were built by Mario Andreschak and communicate with SAP via the same ADT (ABAP Development Tools) API that Eclipse uses under the hood.

Prerequisites

Before starting, you need:

  • Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
  • An SAP ABAP system with /sap/bc/adt active in SICF
  • A user with SAP_ADT_DEVELOPER role (or equivalent)
  • A Claude Pro/Max/Team subscription at claude.ai
  • Internet connection

Step 1 — Install Node.js

Node.js is the runtime required by Claude Code CLI and the MCP servers.

  1. Go to https://nodejs.org and download the LTS version
  2. Install with all defaults
  3. Verify in Command Prompt:
 
 
node -v    → v24.x.xnpm -v     → 11.x.x

Step 2 — Install Git for Windows

Git provides the bash shell Claude Code requires on Windows.

  1. Go to https://git-scm.com/downloads/win and download the installer
  2. During setup, on the PATH screen select: "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"
  3. Complete with all other defaults

Step 3 — Install Claude Code CLI

 
 
cmd
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-codeclaude --version

Then log in:

 
 
cmd
claude

Select option 1 (Claude subscription), log in via browser, return to terminal.

Step 4 — Install mcp-abap-adt (Read-Only MCP Server)

This server gives Claude read access to your SAP system with 13 tools including GetProgram, GetClass, GetFunction, GetTable, SearchObject, and more.

 
 
cmd
mkdir C:\Toolscd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adtcd mcp-abap-adtnpm installnpm run build

Create a .env file in the folder:

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USERNAME=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register with Claude Code:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-adt/dist/index.jsclaude mcp list

You should see: mcp-abap-adt: ✓ Connected

Step 5 — Install mcp-abap-abap-adt-api (Full CRUD Server)

This is the powerful one — 30 tools including read, write, syntax check, activate, lock/unlock, and transport management.

 
 
cmd
cd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api.gitcd mcp-abap-abap-adt-apinpm installnpm run build

Create .env (note: slightly different variable names):

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USER=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300SAP_LANGUAGE=ENNODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt-api node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api/dist/index.js

Step 6 — Setup Claude Desktop (GUI Interface)

Claude Desktop is where it really comes alive — a beautiful chat UI like the LinkedIn video.

  1. Download from https://claude.ai/download
  2. Install and log in
  3. Go to ☰ → Settings → Developer → Edit Config
  4. Replace the content with:
 
 
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "mcp-abap-adt": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-adt\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USERNAME": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    },
    "mcp-abap-adt-api": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-abap-adt-api\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USER": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "SAP_LANGUAGE": "EN",
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    }
  }
}

⚠️ Windows-specific tip: You MUST use the full path C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe — using just "node" does not work in Claude Desktop on Windows. Also note double backslashes \\ in all paths.

  1. Kill Claude in Task Manager and reopen
  2. Go back to Settings → Developer — you should see both servers listed as running ✅

Step 7 — Add Free Official SAP MCP Servers

Bonus: Claude Desktop has a built-in directory of official SAP MCP servers. Click "Connect your tools to Claude" at the bottom of the chat, search for "SAP", and add:

  • SAPUI5 MCP Server — for UI5 development
  • SAP CAP MCP Server — for CAP projects
  • SAP Fiori MCP Server — for Fiori Elements apps

Also add these free hosted servers to Claude Code CLI (no installation needed):

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add --transport http abap-docs https://mcp-abap.marianzeis.de/mcpclaude mcp add --transport http sap-docs https://mcp-sap-docs.marianzeis.de/mcp

These give Claude access to the full ABAP keyword documentation across 8 SAP releases, ABAP cheat sheets, CAP docs, UI5 docs, and SAP Community posts.

Testing It

Open Claude Desktop, create a New Chat, and type:

 
 
Get the source code of program SAPMV45A

Watch Claude automatically call your SAP system, retrieve the source code, and explain it — all in seconds.

For write operations (test on $TMP objects only!):

 
 
Find a simple Z program in $TMP, add a comment at the top saying "Modified by Claude", save and activate it

For complex workflows like the LinkedIn video:

 
 
Connect to my SAP system. Copy class ZCL_SOURCE from package ZPKG_A into ZPKG_B, rename it to ZCL_TARGET, change the carrier_id in the SELECT for /DMO/CONNECTION, create a transport and activate.

Security Notes

  • Never commit .env files to Git
  • Change your SAP password if shared in screenshots
  • Use dedicated service accounts with minimum required authorizations
  • Only test write operations in $TMP before moving to real packages
  • The TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 flag disables SSL validation — use only in dev environments
  • Check your company's AI policy before sending code to external APIs

My Experience

What surprised me most is how Claude handles the entire workflow autonomously. It doesn't just write code — it searches for the object, reads the source, understands the structure, reads class includes, creates the transport, locks the object, writes the code, runs a syntax check, activates, and unlocks. All from one natural language request.

 

Important Hint

It can make ABAP-specific mistakes (like forgetting RAP locking fields, or using reserved DDIC words). Always review AI-generated code before activating in production. But as a development accelerator and code reviewer, it's genuinely powerful.

References

  • GitHub: mcp-abap-adt — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: mcp-abap-abap-adt-api — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: sap-skills — 35 Claude Code plugins for SAP
  • Blog: SAP Docs MCP Server — Marian Zeis
  • Blog: Claude Code via MCP — SAP Community
  • Blog: Installing and Extending an ABAP MCP Server — SAP Community

I hope this helps other ABAP developers get started. If you run into issues or have questions, feel free to comment below. Happy coding!

entry.title: The Quiet Revolution in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Logistics: Clean Core, Transparency, and AI
entry.content:
Supply chains are no longer just about moving physical goods; they are highly synchronized, data-driven ecosystems. As we navigate the latest updates in SAP S/4HANA—particularly with the "progress without disruption" philosophy of the 2025 releases—it is clear that the focus has shifted. We are moving away from reactive workarounds and toward proactive, intelligent, and strictly governed logistics.

For those of us working deep in the trenches of Sourcing, Procurement, and Warehouse Management, this shift brings both exciting capabilities and new operational challenges. Here is a look at the three pillars redefining our day-to-day logistics architecture in the Public Cloud.

1. The Discipline of Clean Core Extensibility

Historically, enforcing specific business rules—like making the "Requisitioner" or "Purchasing Organization" fields mandatory on a Purchase Requisition—often led to messy backend modifications. Today, the Public Cloud enforces a much-needed discipline.

Standard customizing has its limits, but leveraging Key User Extensibility is proving to be a game-changer. By utilizing specific Custom Logic (BAdIs) for Field Control (MM_PUR_S4_PR_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE) and Item Checks (MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK), we can enforce strict data quality right at the entry point of procurement. This approach ensures compliance without compromising the clean core, guaranteeing that our systems remain upgrade-safe and resilient.

2. The Push for Total Process Transparency

True efficiency requires total visibility, yet there are still standard gaps we need to address as a community. A perfect example is workflow simulation.

With release 2602, SAP introduced the highly anticipated ability to simulate workflows for Purchase Orders, allowing teams to validate conditions before documents are processed. However, this same transparency is critically missing for Purchase Requisitions. PRs are the genesis of the procurement lifecycle. Without simulation capabilities at the PR level, organizations are left relying on reactive troubleshooting and trial-and-error. Pushing for these types of intuitive enhancements (like supporting IR #365219) is vital for maintaining process consistency from end to end.

3. Smarter Warehousing and System Resilience

Execution is where strategy meets reality. In the Warehouse Management space, the transition to Public Cloud highlights the need for advanced, automated housekeeping. While traditional EWM robustly handles rearrangement and slotting, Public Cloud WM environments still require creative approaches or scheduled optimization jobs to handle routine bin cleanups and stock consolidation effectively.

As we look toward the future of smart logistics—where predictive maintenance data and real-time equipment health will directly trigger warehouse and procurement tasks—backend stability is non-negotiable. SAP’s recent moves, such as introducing Multi-Availability Zones for the BTP ABAP environment and offering scalable 0.5 ACU runtimes for agile development, provide the exact infrastructure needed to build these high-volume, highly resilient integrations.

Bridging the Gap with AI

Finally, the tools to build this future are evolving faster than ever. From leveraging AI-powered UI5 plugins for rapid application development to integrating Joule into the end-user experience, the technology is ready. The remaining barrier is no longer software; it is the skills gap. As an ecosystem, our next major hurdle is moving past the hype and focusing on the tactical, day-one skills required to actually deploy these AI tools in live logistics environments.

What are your thoughts? Are you finding that Clean Core extensibility is fully meeting your complex procurement needs, or are there still gaps in standard Public Cloud WM functionality that your teams are struggling to work around? Let’s discuss in the comments below!

entry.title: Demo Scenario: Clean Core Extensibility with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build
entry.content:
Co-Authors: @Sivakumar_Subramaniam , @ShivaniShankar 

A common feedback when talking about clean core extensibility is the absence of demo-ready content to visualize these guidelines that have evolved over time. This blog post attempts to take an extensibility scenario as example and showcase the equivalent scenario in a clean-core compliant world. Nevertheless, we cannot claim to be covering every possible flow in one post. The intent is to showcase one example covering a wide range. In the process, we walk through some of the decisions taken that go beyond technology and consider business aspects as well. A lot of technical details are omitted since this post is already quite long. Look forward to the discussions and feedback in the comments section to assess interest and demand for similar posts/more technical versions. 

Recommended Pre-read: 

While an effort is made to abstract technical complexity to a great extent, a reader still needs some basic understanding of the SAP ecosystem, how extensions work, why they exist and some background of clean core.

To maximize takeaways from this blog post, consider reading the clean core extensibility whitepaper before you start, particularly if "Clean Core" sounds unfamiliar. Due to the rich context, answers to why clean core etc available in the whitepaper already, we skip it here. Nevertheless, a lot of what this blog post covers would not make much sense without this background/context. 

Key Assumptions: 

1. We consider level-A of clean core extensibility since this is the highest standard. If one were to target level-B for example, the target architecture might look a little different with potential reuse of certain objects

2. For simplicity and to keep the focus on extensibility, we assume there is no fit-to-standard solution available for this scenario which may or may not be factually correct. In a real project, please consider fit-to-standard analysis and an analysis of your existing custom-developed artefacts before developing something new

Scenario Description : 
- Provide a capability to add custom gift cards when creating sales orders in SAP Cloud ERP Private (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition)

- On applying the gift card, a discount is added to the sales order by an amount equal to the gift card amount

- On creation of a sales order, loyalty points need to get accrued against the membership of the business partner (from the sales order). For simplicity, assume only business partners of type "Customer"

- The loyalty program has tiers and based on the tier the customer is on, he/she/they accrue a percentage of the sales order value as points

- It shall be possible for a customer to request an upgrade to their loyalty program tier. In this case, approval from an admin is required before the change is active 

Scenario Refinement:  

Understanding/context: When working with sales orders, this company has 2 parallel reward programs. One, a gift card that can be applied on creation of the sales order for an immediate discount. Another, a loyalty program where one accrues points with each sales order. Potentially, for redemption (not in scope here) through different means at a later point in time

Tasks:

- Since gift cards need to be applied, there is also an unsaid need to be able to create and maintain these gift cards (by an admin). This means an app to create/maintain gift cards with appropriate fields

- To be able to apply the gift card when creating a sales order, an option is needed directly in the sales order application (or transaction)

- The applied gift card should impact the value of the sales order before creation (not post-processing) 

- Similarly, loyalty program tiers would need to be maintained (possibly as business configuration)

- The loyalty points are calculated based on the created sales order and don't have a dependency or need to update/modify the consistency of the sales order itself. This could be handled as a separate activity from the creation of the sales order

Solution Architecture (Classic Extensibility - non-Clean Core level-A):In the classic extensibility scenario which is non-clean core level-A, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - WebDynPro Application
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in VA01 (Transaction code for sales order creation) - User Exit
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Module Pool Program
  4. Tier upgrade - Classic workflow

 Solution Architecture (with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build - Clean Core)

In the clean-core level-A compliant extensibility scenario, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - Application developed using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) with on-stack developer extensibility. Yes! with real code written by a developer inside the S/4HANA system outside of key user tools and still the highest level of clean core! If this is against your understanding of clean core, read SAP's official whitepaper (section 3.2 at the time this blog post is written) linked in the pre-read section of this blog post!  
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in the standard sales order Fiori app  - Key User Extensibility
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Side-by-side extension using BTP ABAP Environment
  4. Maintenance of tiers of the loyalty program - Business Configuration in the BTP ABAP Environment (not shown in the demo except as value help)
  5. Tier upgrade - SAP Build Process Automation
  6. Communication between S/4HANA and BTP - Event Mesh (or Advanced Event Mesh)

Alternate options - The Loyalty Points Application could also have been developed side-by-side using CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) with additional handling for tier maintenance. 

Decision Criteria (Technical and Business): 

  1. The use case around application of gift card has a very tight dependency on the sales order. The standard SAP sales order application needs to understand and bring up the gift cards for a value help, have the gift cards applied consistently across impacted business objects when the sales order is updated. All of this needs to happen as part of the sales order creation/update process in S/4HANA. This makes on-stack developer extensibility and key-user extensibility good candidates. 
  2. The loyalty points application has 2 factors to consider - Side-by-side v/s on-stack and CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
    • Side-by-Side v/s On-Stack:
      • Decision: Side-by-Side
      • In private cloud/on-premise, SAP ships a new major version every 2 years. By moving out of the core (S/4HANA) anything that can be moved logically without adverse performance/over-engineering, etc, the customer can benefit from always being on the latest release and getting the latest updates. This de-risks innovation from upgrade project timelines and upgrade strategy. 
      • Enforcement of clean core - With the side-by-side approach, clean core is enforced with syntax checks etc since ABAP Cloud is the only option on BTP ABAP Environment as opposed to governance based approaches on-stack which may need an ABAP Cloud package and processes to ensure a developer doesn't slip back to the familiar classic extensibility when cloud ready alternatives are available.
    • CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
      • Decision: Both options are valid. Here, ABAP Cloud due to developer skill set and business configuration handling know-how. Since each option has strengths and benefits, refer the BTP Developer's guide for details. To generalize any of the two as always the better option without case-to-case analysis and comparison may be an unfair/sub-optimal decision. 

General question on BTP ABAP Environment cost -
"The cost of BTP ABAP Environment is higher" - Apart from the smaller runtime size now available, which reduces costs, the cost needs to be spread out across applications being developed. One instance of the BTP ABAP Environment can be reused for multiple applications so long as the runtime/memory requirements are within available limits. Compare this to additional costs for CF Runtime (for example) for each deployed app with CAP and the costing at solution level at scale tends to be similar. This is not to say CAP is a bad option, just that when the decision is influenced by cost, how this cost is calculated decides quite a bit! When this becomes equivalent, the models can be compared on best fit for the use case on technical/architectural grounds.

Demo of functional scenario in both approaches along with ATC findings across levels - B, C, D on the package where classic extensibility was used

The following demo showcases the scenario with classic extensibility as well as with Clean Core Level-A. It also touches upon ATC checks on the package containing the classic extension. To understand how ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) helps categorize the findings, refer section 3.3.4(at the time of writing) of the clean core extensibility whitepaper (in the pre-read section)

 

Call to Action: Comment below what you would wish was better covered in the post and what key takeaways you had! Your inputs help us understand and validate where such blog posts can add more value

entry.title: Business partner Master data change history
entry.content:
Introduction:- 

In modern SAP systems, the concept of the Business Partner (BP) plays a central and combining role in managing master data. SAP introduced the Business Partner approach to simplify data maintenance and improve consistency across applications.  
 
A Business Partner in SAP S/4HANA represents any Person, organization, or group with whom a company conducts business. This includes customers, vendors, employees, and other . SAP puts all roles into one place, reducing duplicates and keeping data accurate. 

One important thing about this Business Partner management is change history tracking. In real-world business scenarios, master data is frequently updated—such as address changes, bank details, or role assignments.   
 
In this blog, we will explore why change history may not appear and what causes it, using simple examples. 

Things You Should Know About Change History in Business Partner:- 

When you need to track changes made to a business partner, you can use the change history feature to view and display those updates clearly. 

We use two tables for change history: CDHDR (Change History Header) and CDPOS (Change History Item). 

In CDHDR, you need to pass the business partner number in the Object ID field, which will display all the related changes. 

In this blog, we will explore the various reasons why change history may not appear in the business partner (BP), using simple and practical examples.

Create a business partner (BP) and make some changes to it. In this example, I have updated the Search Term field from #128 to #127. 

You can check the change history in a business partner (BP) in two ways: 
Extras → Change History → For This Partner: 

This displays all the changes that have been made to the business partner. 
Extras → Change History → For This Field: 

This displays the changes related to a specific field only. 

Extras → Change History → For This Partner: 
This displays the change history as shown below. Since we only updated the Search Term field, it shows changes related to that field only. 

Sometimes, even after making changes, they are not captured in the change history, and no records are stored in the CDHDR or CDPOS tables. 

First, check the BUT000 (General Data of Business Partner) table for the field where changes are not being recorded. Then, verify whether the “Change Document” option is enabled in the data element, as shown below. Make sure this option is active so that change history can be stored properly. 

Second, you need to check the field name in the BP transaction using F1 help and then go to Technical Information. This helps you identify the correct technical field details. 

Then, verify the corresponding field name in the BUT000 table. 

Then, check the field group for that field. Click on the field name and, in the T-code search bar, enter BDT_ANALYZER and press enter . 

Then identify the field group for NAME_ORG1 as follows: 
Sections → BUP010 - Name → BUP200 - Organization → Field Groups → 0045 - Organization: Name 1 
So, the field group for NAME_ORG1 is 0045. 

Now go to transaction BUS11 and check whether field group 45 is maintained for NAME_ORG1. This ensures that the field can be fetched through the BAPI and displayed in the output.
 
Sometimes, the field is not maintained in the BUS11 transaction, and in such cases, the change history will not be retrieved for that field, so it will not appear in the output. 

Thirdly, go to transaction BUS7 (Display View "Events": Overview). 

  • Select the CHGD1 event and navigate to Events → Function Module Allocation to view the assigned function modules.  
  • For function modules BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD1 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD1 (Applications BUA and BUB), ensure that the “Call” column is marked with ‘X’.  
  • Navigate back (F3).  

Repeat the same steps for the events CHGD2, CHGD3, and CHGD4, and verify the corresponding function modules: 

  • CHGD2 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD2 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD2  
  • CHGD3 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD3 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD3  
  • CHGD4 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD4 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD4  
  • Finally, save the changes on the screen . 

We have the report BUSCHDOC to view the change history for multiple business partners. Using this report, we can display change history for a large number of BPs efficiently. 

Additionally, the BPCD transaction is available for the BUSCHDOC report. It generates the selection screen based on the application object and provides a multi-screen report to analyze business partner change documents. 

Prerequisites to execute BUSCHDOC report:- 
Sometimes in customer systems, the BUSCHDOC report may not display the output screen. To make the output visible, you need to ensure the following: 

BUPSELG5 and BUPSELG0 reports are used to generate the select options for the reports BUSCHDOC for a given application object (Eg: BUPA). 

Execution of these reports is a prerequisite for execution of BUSCHDOC  reports. First report BUPSELG5 should be executed then BUPSELG0. 

Please note these reports are language specific and must be executed once for each logon language. 

Many times, we may encounter a “TIME_OUT” dump while executing this report, especially when processing millions of business partners to display change history. 

Resolution:- 
Business Partner is the leading object in S/4 systems, and the number of applications consuming BP has been increasing with each service pack release. To determine BP changes, all registered applications must be called, and their responses consolidated to generate the output. As the number of applications grows, the processing time also increases. 

Since running BUSCHDOC for all BPs is not a regular daily activity, it is recommended to split the range into variants and execute the report as a background job. 

The second approach is to split the process into parallel jobs instead of running a single job with multiple variants, which can help reduce the overall execution time. However, in this case, you will need to consolidate the outputs from all job logs. 

Simple example to use BUSCHDOC report:- 
This is the BUSCHDOC report output screen. Here, we need to enter BUPA as the application object. 

While executing the BUSCHDOC report in the foreground, it is mandatory to enter the Business Partner number. 

Since entering the Business Partner number is mandatory, we are passing 154-12071. 

If no Business Partner number is entered, the system will display the following popup. Therefore, it is important to always provide a BP range to avoid a runtime dump. 

Output: Change history for BP 154-12071.

Conclusion:- 
In my project, validating SAP Business Partner change document settings improved data transparency and helped quickly identify missing logs using BUS7, BUS11, and BUSCHDOC (BPCD). This reduced troubleshooting effort and sped up issue resolution.

Handling performance with background runs and BP ranges avoided timeouts and ensured smooth processing. Overall, it made monitoring and support more efficient.

If you’ve encountered similar situations or have different approaches, feel free to share your thoughts or ask questions in the comments!

entry.title: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic!
entry.content:
Summary

  • SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition in all releases as of 2021 with planned start in Q2/2026
  • SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be priced on a consumption basis via AI Units
  • ABAP Development Tools for VS Code are planned to be GA in Q2/2026
  • An ABAP MCP Server will be shipped in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and ABAP Development Tools for VS Code as of Q2/2026 enabling agentic ABAP development experience
  • An ABAP Custom Code Transformation agent focusing on the migration from SAP ECC towards SAP S/4HANA is planned for Q2/2026

Intro

Since the start of ABAP AI in early 2025 (“Joule speaks ABAP!”), the update in September 2025 (“ABAP AI – Chapter 2”), now it is time to deliver on the promises made at SAP TechEd 2025 (“Introducing the next era of ABAP development”) – time for chapter 3!

We are entering the agentic era of ABAP development: let's get started.

SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities in lower releases

As of June 2026, Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities are offered through a running on SAP BTP. This hub service combines authentication, license check, access to SAP BTP AI Core and use case specific business logic and can be plugged directly to the developer’s IDE. This way, we can offer ABAP AI capabilities mostly independent of the ABAP backend release. This enables us to serve SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition on releases 2021 and higher with Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26
  • Supported releases: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2021 and higher
  • Costs: See below
  • Configuration guide and further information will follow and will be linked here

New commercial approach for ABAP AI

The free promotion for J4D ABAP AI capabilities comes to an end in September 2026. Accordingly, it is now time to launch the commercial model: Based on AI units, ABAP AI capabilities are now priced purely based on consumption. This approach combines transparency regarding the real costs based on the usage and is well-suited for dynamic and individual agentic AI use cases.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26
  • Materials: 8019164 (customers) and 8019670 (partners)
  • Information on how to purchase and activate will follow soon and will be linked here

ABAP Development Tools for VS Code

14 years after the launch of ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, time has come to support a new IDE. As Q2 2026, ABAP Development Tools will be available for VS Code. Building on the ABAP Language Server (see “Behind the Design…”), coupled with an ABAP MCP Server, this is the enabler for a truly agentic ABAP development experience, boosting development efficiency to the next level. It sets the foundation to leverage modern coding agents like GitHub Copilot directly for ABAP development. While we start with a small set of development objects and IDE tools focusing on SAP Fiori App development based on the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), we will very soon enhance the scope of object types to serve high priority development scenarios.

The VS Code extension is

  • planned to be available as of Q2/26
  • Supported releases: release 7.31 and higher
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Costs: free of charge
  • Precise info on how to obtain and get started will follow soon and will be linked here

MCP Support for ABAP development

The Language Server provides the required abstraction to gain independence of the IDE sitting on top. A major component of this delivery is the MCP server for ABAP development. It provides powerful tools which help coding agents to perform complex ABAP coding tasks. While the Language Server is currently delivered as part of the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and for VS Code, a potential standalone-shipment of the Language and MCP Server is under discussion.

  • Release planned for Q2/26
  • Supported in Eclipse and VS Code
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Supported for release 7.31 and higher
  • Precise info on how to use it in the IDE will follow soon and will be linked here

Agentic custom code migration for the SAP S/4HANA transformation

Bringing all pieces together, we are launching the first ABAP custom code agent assisting in the transformation of custom code from ECC towards SAP S/4HANA. The required capabilities are offered through the ABAP Language and MCP Server explained above and can be used leveraging a coding agent, for example Amazon Q or GitHub Copilot. Currently, Eclipse must be used for this agent, until all required ABAP object types are supported in VS Code (see above). This agent boosts our customers on the RISE journey when migrating their classic landscape to the Cloud ERP.

  • Availability planned for Q2/26.
  • Supported in Eclipse
  • Prerequisites: Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities via the new commercial approach with AI units (see above) and coding agent on top (e.g. Amazon Q or Github Copilot)
  • Further information to follow soon and will be linked here

 

Finally - please have a look at our FAQ document for frequent questions and watch out for the GA releases of these deliverables. We will keep you posted.

Get yourself ready to dive into agentic ABAP development! 😎

entry.title: Announcement: Register to the new CEI project for SAP Application Interface Framework
entry.content:
SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) is an integral part of SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud for implementing, monitoring, and managing application interfaces.

In production environments, when a large number of interface messages fail, manual error diagnosis and error resolution can lead to significant effort and time. To address this, we plan to enhance SAP Application Interface Framework to support an AI-assisted error remediation framework that transforms error handling from tedious manual work into intelligent, automated recovery.

To gather feedback on those planned product improvements, we will run the Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) project AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework. The CEI project aims to collect feedback on the concept and a first proof of concept at an early stage, focusing on:

  • Error classification: How AI-driven error classification can reduce manual effort.
  • Remediation automation: Validating the remediation approach against real customer scenarios.
  • Autonomous vs. guided remediation: Understanding customer preferences for automatic execution versus human-in-the-loop approval.

If you like to join the upcoming SAP Application Interface Framework influence project, please register here (link leads to the customer influence page).

Registration is open from May 18th until June 21st 2026.

Once, the registration closes, we will invite you for an initial call where we go through the scope and the prerequisites of the feedback project. This will be around end of June, begin of July, the actual feedback sessions will start in July.

We are looking forward to your active participation and valuable feedback.

entry.title: How to Send Email for Maintenance Orders Generated by Maintenance Plans in S/4HANA Cloud 2602
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Cloud 2602, there are multiple ways to trigger email notifications when preventive maintenance orders are generated from maintenance plan.

This blog outlines two approaches: one configuration driven (recommended) and one extensibility driven to help you choose the right solution based on your requirements and Clean Core strategy.

Business Requirement

Alert relevant users to their company email address when a maintenance order is generated from a maintenance plan so that they can review and take action promptly.

Option 1: Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders (Recommended)

The standard and Clean Core–aligned approach is to use the Fiori app:

F4989 – Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders:

🔧Configuration Steps

  • Define Global Preconditions Set workflow trigger conditions such as:
    • Order Type (Preventive)
    • Maintenance Plan ≠ empty (ensures trigger from IP10/IP30)
    • Planning Plant / Planner Group (optional filters)
  • Add Workflow Step
    • Step Type: Notification Only
    • Recipients:
      • Partner on order
      • Planner group
      • Person responsible
      • Specific users
    • Execution:
      • Enable auto-completion in My Inbox
  • Email Notification Setup:
    • Use Fiori app: Manage Email Templates
    • Copy standard template: SWT_CRT_NOTIFY_RECIPIENTS
    • Follow naming convention: YY_02000019_CRT_10
    • Maintain template content:
      • Subject: Alert: Maintenance Order {{OrderNumber}} from PM is generated
      • Body: Maintenance Order {{OrderNumber}} has been generated via Maintenance Plan for Technical Object {{TechnicalObject}}. Please review your technical log.

Prerequisites:

  • User email maintained in:
    • Maintain Business Users
    • Manage Business Partners
  • Preventive order type:
    • Assigned to a Phase Model Profile
    • Workflow trigger based on status change (e.g., Approval)

Key Benefits

  • Fully aligned with SAP Clean Core principles
  • Configuration-driven (no coding required)
  • Business-maintainable and audit-ready
  • Upgrade-safe and scalable

Option 2: BAdI-Based Enhancement (Advanced Scenario)

For scenarios requiring more advanced logic, you can use ABAP Cloud extensibility.

🔧Technical Approach

Implement the cloud-released BAdI: BADI_EAM_MO_CH_BEF_SAVE

Trigger Logic:

  • Execute logic when:
    • Order Type = Preventive
    • Maintenance Plan ≠ empty (indicates IP10/IP30 origin)
    • Optional filters:
      • Planning Plant
      • Planner Group

Email Implementation

  • Use modern email API: CL_BCS_MAIL_MESSAGE

Key Implementation Steps

  • Set sender: .set_sender()
  • Add recipients dynamically: .add_recipient() (e.g., fetch from I_MaintenanceOrder CDS view)
  • Define subject: .set_subject()
  • Send email asynchronously: .send_asynchronous()
  • Helps avoid transactional impact or rollback issues

Key Benefits

  • Full flexibility in:
    • Additional Custom logic as per business needs
    • Recipient determination
    • Email content

Considerations

  • Requires ABAP Cloud (Tier 1 extensibility)
  • Higher development and maintenance effort
  • Needs governance to stay compliant with Clean Core strategy

Recommendation

  • Use Flexible Workflow (Option 1) as the default approach
  • Consider BAdI (Option 2) only when:
    • Workflow limitations prevent required logic
    • Advanced recipient or filtering logic is needed

Final Thoughts

  • For most use cases, Flexible Workflow provides a robust, scalable, and upgrade-safe solution aligned with SAP’s Clean Core strategy.
  • BAdI-based enhancement should be used selectively for advanced scenarios where additional flexibility is required.

Hope this helps!
